categoryDescription: Evaluates the ETF's risk profile — how bumpy the ride is, how bad the worst losses have been, and (where the angle is genuinely additive for that group) interest-rate sensitivity, credit risk, daily-reset decay, downside-protection / capture behavior, and concentration risk. Pass/Fail is always mandate-based: a high-beta thematic fund is expected to swing, a low-vol fund is expected to be calm, a covered-call fund is expected to trade upside for downside protection, and a managed-futures fund is expected to decorrelate from equities — each is judged against its own job, not against a broad-equity benchmark. Factors are assigned only to the groups where the factor adds a non-overlapping angle — a group may map to fewer factors when the rest would just repeat what the core volatility / Sharpe / drawdown trio already says. Each factor's `factorAnalysisDescription` carries the generic measurement principle and the mandate-based Pass/Fail rule; `groupInstructions` carries the 1-2 line group-specific perspective.

factors.factorDescription: Measures how much the ETF's price moves day-to-day and month-to-month, using beta (sensitivity vs the broad market, typically S&P 500 for equity / multi-asset funds) and standard deviation (size of typical return swings). A beta of 1.0 means the fund moves roughly with the market; above 1.2 means amplified swings in both directions; below 0.8 means calmer moves. Interpretation must match the mandate: a 'low-volatility' equity fund with beta near 1.0 is failing at its stated job; a 3x leveraged fund SHOULD have beta near 3.0 on the underlying; a multi-sector credit fund has near-zero equity beta by design. For young funds (under 2 years), beta is noisy — flag the short history and lean on category comparison instead of a single number. Pass when the volatility profile fits the fund's mandate and category norm. Fail when volatility is materially mismatched to mandate (low-vol label with market-like beta, leveraged fund far off its target multiple, or volatility well above category peers without a strategy explanation).
factors.factorMetrics: beta, beta1y, beta2y, beta5y, atr, riskAndVolatilityMeasures

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Beta near 1.0 vs S&P 500 is the baseline for US large blend / total market; style tilts should show the matching beta deviation (low-vol below 0.8, growth above 1.0 in growth cycles, dividend / value typically 0.85-1.0). Pass when beta and stdev fit the tilt's label across multiple windows; a 'min-vol' fund whose beta sits near 1.0 is failing its job regardless of absolute return.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Beta vs S&P 500 typically runs 1.0-1.4 for cyclicals and tech, below 1.0 for defensive sectors (consumer staples, utilities, healthcare). Single-country EM funds carry currency-driven extra volatility on top of the equity-beta read. Judge against the sector's historical character and peers, not against the broad-equity baseline.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Beta should sit close to the stated daily-leverage multiple on the underlying (3x equity fund → beta near 3.0 against the underlying; -2x inverse → beta near -2.0). Any large deviation signals daily-tracking breakdown. The retail message: high volatility is BY DESIGN here, not a flaw.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Equity-S&P beta is the right correlation read (gold beta near zero is the diversification claim; BTC beta has run 0.6-0.8 in recent years), but standard deviation of monthly returns tells the real volatility story — gold 12-18% annualized, broad commodities 15-25%, crypto frequently above 60%. A gold fund showing S&P-like beta has lost its diversification value.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Group dispersion is wide — covered-call funds typically run 50-70% of S&P vol; market-neutral and managed-futures often run 20-40%; long-short equity sits between. Judge against the specific sub-strategy, not the whole group; a market-neutral fund with S&P-like vol is failing its job.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Vol should slot into the stated risk band — conservative allocation 4-8% stdev, moderate 8-12%, aggressive 12-16%; target-date glide paths should show declining vol over time as bonds replace equities. Beta vs S&P should reflect the equity weight (~0.6 for moderate, ~0.85 for aggressive).
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Equity-market beta is misleading — judge via standard deviation of monthly returns and the riskAndVolatilityMeasures block. Short / ultrashort funds run 1-3% annualized stdev; intermediate core 4-7%; long-duration government / muni 10-15%; money market essentially zero.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Equity-beta matters here in a way it does not for IG — HY corporate runs 0.4-0.6 equity beta, bank loans 0.2-0.4, preferred 0.5-0.7, convertibles often 0.6+ (more equity than bond). Standard deviation 6-12% is typical; readings well above this band signal a higher-risk credit mix than the label suggests.

factors.factorTitle: Are You Paid Fairly for the Risk
factors.factorDescription: Measures how much return investors earned per unit of risk taken, using Sharpe (excess return ÷ total volatility) and Sortino (excess return ÷ downside volatility). Higher is better, but the threshold is category-specific. For active funds Sharpe is the honest test of whether the manager's picks added real risk-adjusted value; for passive funds Sharpe vs category tells you whether the index itself was an efficient exposure over that window. For young funds (under 3 years) Sharpe is unreliable and the short history should be stated explicitly. Leveraged / inverse funds are deliberately excluded — multi-year Sharpe is essentially meaningless when daily-reset decay destroys the risk/return relationship. Pass when Sharpe is at or above the category median over the longest available multi-year window AND Sortino is consistent with Sharpe (no hidden downside story). Fail when Sharpe is materially below category median over a multi-year window without a mandate-based explanation.

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Broad-equity Sharpe above 0.5 is decent and above 1.0 is very good over a multi-year window. Compare to the S&P 500's Sharpe in the same window — a passive Large Blend should be within tracking distance; an active or tilt fund should show whether the tilt actually paid for the extra risk it took.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector Sharpe is more volatile than broad-equity Sharpe — read multi-year context (tech and discretionary lead in growth cycles, defensives in late-cycle). Do not Fail a sector fund for the wrong-half-of-cycle Sharpe; the honest test is whether this Sharpe is at or above its sector-peer median over a multi-year window.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Sharpe varies enormously by window (BTC has swung from very high to deeply negative inside two years; gold ranges roughly 0.2 to 0.8 over multi-year windows). Read multi-year context and judge against the asset's own history, not against equity Sharpe.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Derivative-income and hedge-style funds can show low or even negative Sharpe during equity bull markets yet still deliver on a crisis-hedging mandate — judge against category peers, not against broad equity. A managed-futures fund with negative Sharpe over 2017-2021 followed by strong 2022 performance was doing its job.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Sharpe of 0.5-1.0 is typical for allocation and target-date funds — a moderate-allocation fund matching 60/40 returns with lower vol is doing its job even if absolute return lags pure equity. Compare to a passive 60/40 same-period Sharpe.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Sharpe is structurally lower (0.2-0.5 is normal) because both excess return and volatility are compressed. For passive Treasury / IG funds, Sharpe vs category tells you whether the index itself was an efficient exposure — do not Fail a passive fund for matching its index's Sharpe.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Sharpe is usually 0.3-0.6 mid-cycle and can go deeply negative in credit shock years (2008, 2020 partial). Multi-year Sharpe matters more than single-year; compare to the matching credit benchmark's Sharpe in the same window.

factors.factorTitle: Worst Drop and How Long It Took to Recover
factors.factorDescription: Shows the biggest peak-to-trough fall the ETF has had and, where the data allows, how long it took to recover the prior high. The retail question is emotional — could an investor actually hold through that drop without selling at the bottom? The comparative test matters more than the absolute number: a fund that fell harder AND recovered slower than its category peers is the real red flag; a fund that fell in line with peers was hit by the asset class, not by a fund-specific flaw. Young funds with no 2020 or 2022 test may under-report true downside — flag this gap and judge by strategy design and peer analogues. Pass when drawdown sits within the normal range for the category and recovery behavior was not materially slower than peers. Fail when drawdown is materially deeper than category peers during the same stress window, recovery has been notably slower while peers bounced back, or the fund's structural design (concentrated single-name or single-country exposure) magnified the drawdown beyond what the category label suggests.
factors.factorMetrics: drawdown, drawdownDates, athChgPercent, athDate, atlChgPercent, atlDate

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Broad-equity drawdowns of -20% to -35% hit roughly once a decade (2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock, 2008 GFC). For foreign-developed and global funds, reference MSCI EAFE's -16% in 2022 and -45% in 2008. Compare to the tracked index — within tolerance is Pass; a passive fund cannot fairly be Failed for matching its index's drawdown.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector and thematic funds drop -40% to -60% in a bad sector year (tech 2022, energy 2020, biotech 2022); EM funds drew down -30% to -45% in 2008 and 2022. Single-country EM (China, India, Brazil) can deepen the drawdown to -50%+. Frame the worst year against both the sector index and the S&P 500.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: 3x leveraged funds have routinely drawn down -70% to -90% in bad years and recovered slowly or not at all. The mechanical expectation is leverage × underlying drawdown minus daily-reset slippage; show the underlying's drawdown alongside so retail sees the arithmetic. Recovery often requires the underlying to print a new all-time high, sometimes never within a reasonable holding period.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Gold has had multi-year drawdowns near -45%; broad commodity funds drew down -20% to -30% in 2020; spot BTC has had multiple -75% to -83% drawdowns. Compare to the spot underlying's drawdown — a material gap suggests a tracking issue or futures-roll cost story.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Covered-call funds typically draw down 50-70% as deep as their underlying (JEPI -13% vs S&P -25% in 2022 is 'mandate delivered'); long-short and market-neutral often hold within -10% in equity bear markets when working correctly. Compare to both the underlying equity benchmark and the strategy peer median.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Moderate allocation drew down -16% to -18% in 2022 (S&P -25%); aggressive -22% to -25%; conservative -10% to -13%. Target-date dated-year funds approaching retirement (2025-2030 vintages) lost less than long-dated (2055-2065) — that is the glide path working as designed.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: 2022 was the rate-shock test — long government / muni (TLT, MUB Long) lost -25% to -31%; intermediate core (AGG) ~-13%; short / ultrashort ~-3%; MMF essentially flat. Compare worst year to a duration-matched Treasury index — within tolerance is Pass.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: 2020 COVID hit HY -15-20%, bank loans -5-10%, EM debt -15%, preferreds -15-25%; 2022 hit longer-duration credit harder. 2008 GFC was the deeper credit stress — HYG drew down ~-22%. Compare to the matching credit benchmark; in-line with index is Pass.

factors.factorTitle: How Much Rate Changes Hurt This Fund
factors.factorDescription: Interest-rate risk: rates up → bond prices down, and the effect is multiplied by duration (weighted time to cash flow). The same mechanism works in reverse — funds that lost most in 2022 gained most in 2019-2020 falling-rate years. The retail takeaway is to match the fund's duration to the investor's holding horizon and rate view; long-duration is a directional rate bet. Compare the fund's 2022 (or other rate-shock) loss to the category and index — if the loss was in line, the drawdown was asset-class-driven, not a fund-specific flaw. For young funds without a 2022 data point, judge by stated duration vs category norms and the fund's rolling volatility pattern. Pass when the rate-shock loss was in line with category peers for the fund's stated duration bucket, OR current duration positioning is within the fund's mandate band. Fail when the fund lost materially more than category peers in rate-shock years without an offsetting credit / yield advantage, OR when current duration positioning is a large unannounced bet retail holders cannot easily see.
factors.factorMetrics: duration, beta, beta1y, beta2y, beta5y, riskAndVolatilityMeasures, riskPeriods

groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Long-duration government / muni (15Y+) lost -25% to -31% in 2022; intermediate core (5-7Y) -10% to -15%; short / ultrashort (<3Y) a few percent; MMF essentially flat. Long-duration is a directional rate bet; ultrashort behaves like cash. For muni, AMT exposure and state-specific issuer rules apply but the duration mechanism is the same.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Rate sensitivity comes through the bond sleeve — a moderate-allocation fund with a 40% bond sleeve inherits roughly 40% of that sleeve's duration loss in a rate shock. 2022 hit aggressive allocation -25% (mostly equity) but moderate -16% (the bond sleeve added meaningful pain). Compare to a same-sleeve passive 60/40.

factors.factorTitle: Risk That Bonds in the Fund Default
factors.factorDescription: Measures the risk that bonds inside the ETF default or get downgraded, judged via credit-quality breakdown and behavior during credit-stress periods (2008 GFC, 2020 COVID). The stress-test is comparative: losses materially worse than credit-peer category in stress windows suggest excess exposure to lower-rated or concentrated issuers the fund label does not make obvious. The longer-dated members (preferred, EM debt, convertibles) carry duration alongside credit — when discussing drawdowns make clear whether the loss was credit-driven (2020), rate-driven (2022), or both. Pass when the credit-tier mix and stress behavior are in line with category norms and the fund's stated credit mandate. Fail when 2020 losses were materially worse than category peers, OR the credit-mix is reaching for yield beyond what the labeled category implies.

groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Credit-quality tiers: HY corporate (BB and below) typically loses -15% to -25% in recessions (HYG -22% in 2008); bank loans are senior-secured floating-rate (~70¢ recovery rate limits loss-given-default); CLO funds depend on tranche rating (AAA behaved like floating-rate IG in 2020; sub-IG behaved like HY); preferred is subordinated, bond-like in rates but equity-like in crises (PFF -18% in 2022); EM debt layers credit + currency + sovereign risk (USD-denominated removes the currency leg); convertibles are subordinated and equity-correlated. High-yield muni carries real default risk in revenue-bond / lower-rated state issuers.

factors.factorDescription: Daily-reset leveraged and inverse ETFs reset their leverage target every day, which mathematically guarantees that long-term returns are NOT the leverage factor times the underlying's long-term return. In flat or choppy markets daily compounding destroys value (volatility drag); in strongly trending markets it helps. This factor is partly a structural warning — every product in the group carries it by design — so the retail takeaway to reinforce is constant: not buy-and-hold regardless of Pass/Fail. What distinguishes good from bad: daily-leverage tracking tightness, issuer transparency on holding-period risk, minimal bid-ask slippage given the rapid trading these funds require, no history of catastrophic structural failures. Pass when daily fidelity is tight, the fund is clearly marketed as short-term, there is no history of structural blowups, and category-expected decay is disclosed. Fail when daily tracking has broken down beyond category norms, structural issues have produced outsized losses in a specific stress event, or liquidity / bid-ask makes intended short-term trading impractical.
factors.factorMetrics: return1m, return3m, return1y, return3y, returnsAnnual, beta, beta1y

groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Test daily-leverage fidelity: a well-run 3x should match 3× the underlying's daily return within a few bps. Past blowups to watch for: inverse oil funds in April 2020 when crude went negative, inverse-volatility products in February 2018 (the XIV unwind). Reinforce the retail takeaway every time: short-term trading vehicle, not buy-and-hold; holding-period risk grows with volatility regardless of which way the underlying moves.

factors.factorDescription: The asymmetric-payoff test — does the fund actually deliver less downside than its mandate-relevant benchmark, ideally without giving up all of the upside? Two complementary lenses, one factor: (a) upside / downside capture ratios over the available history (how much of benchmark up-months vs down-months the fund caught), and (b) drawdown vs benchmark in actual stress windows (2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock, 2008 GFC where applicable). Rough interpretation: a downside-capture or stress-drawdown ratio below 0.50 is excellent (fund fell less than half as much as benchmark); 0.50-0.75 is good; 0.75-0.90 is modest; above 0.90 means the fund is taking essentially the full ride down and is not providing meaningful protection for its fees. Young funds without a bear-market test must be judged provisionally using available capture data with the caveat stated. Pass when the capture pattern and stress drawdown match the fund's mandate (asymmetric favorable for defensive strategies, decorrelated for alt, balanced-and-smaller for allocation, equity-uncorrelated for true diversifiers). Fail when a defensive / income-strategy / allocation / diversifier fund shows no meaningful downside advantage over a plain-vanilla passive, when a buffer fund failed to clip downside at its labeled buffer, or when a covered-call fund gave up upside without delivering the promised cushion.

groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Gold typically shows positive or near-flat returns in equity drawdowns of -10%+ (the classic diversifier behavior); broad commodities are more mixed; BTC has correlated with risk-on / risk-off equities post-2022. Test the diversification claim against the actual 2020, 2022, and 2008-style drawdown windows — a 'diversifier' that fell with equities in 2022 has not delivered.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Covered-call funds (JEPI, QYLD, SPYI) should typically show 60-80% upside capture and 50-70% downside capture. If upside is near 100%, the options program is not binding (no real income generation); if downside is near 100%, the options program is not providing the protection it charges for. Defined-outcome buffer funds should clip downside at the labeled buffer (-10%, -15%) for the outcome period AND limit upside to the printed cap. Managed-futures / market-neutral / long-short funds often show very low capture on both sides (under 40%) — that is the low-correlation mandate delivering, not a flaw; for those, the honest test is positive or near-flat returns in equity bear markets.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: A 60/40 portfolio that fell -20% when stocks fell -20% failed at its core mandate — -12% to -14% would have been in line. Test against the dated-year peer norm; target-date 2025-2030 vintages should have meaningfully smaller drawdowns than 2055-2065 vintages. The fee question matters: if the fund charges 50bps+ for allocation services, the smoothing should be visible in the capture numbers.

factors.factorTitle: Risk of Being All-In on One Theme
factors.factorDescription: Sector, thematic, and single-country / regional emerging-market ETFs deliberately concentrate — that is the product — but within-bucket concentration is where the real hidden risk sits. Check three things: (1) top-10 holdings weight — below ~40% is well-diversified within the theme, 40-60% is typical, above 60% means the fund's fate is tied to a handful of names; (2) single-name maximum — any position above 10% is meaningful single-stock risk disguised as a sector / thematic bet (several semi funds carry around 15% in NVDA); (3) sub-sector, single-country, or currency concentration within a theme. Always reinforce the retail takeaway: these belong as small slices of a portfolio, not the whole thing. Pass when within-theme diversification is reasonable for the category — no single-name above ~15%, top-10 weight not above category average — AND the fund's drawdown history during sector down-cycles was not materially worse than its sector-peer group. Fail when single-name concentration exceeds ~15% combined with 'diversified' marketing language, OR top-10 concentration is meaningfully higher than category average and the fund has shown outsized drawdowns because of it.

groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Tech and discretionary funds often run 50%+ top-10 weight with single-name above 10% (NVDA, MSFT, AAPL); ESG and clean-energy funds often have hidden sub-sector concentration (mostly US solar / EV battery); single-country EM funds can be 30%+ concentrated in a few names (a 'China' fund mostly Hong Kong-listed internet; a 'Diversified EM' fund 30%+ Taiwan via TSMC). Frame the worst sector drawdown alongside the concentration metrics so retail sees the connection.
